title: US–IRAN CEASEFIRE
verdict_detail: Most likely path: Omani-mediated direct deal between senior US and Iranian officials.
verdict_context: Multiple diplomatic streams have shifted since UNSC Resolution 2891 (Mar 8) established a new inspection framework. Oman hosted the highest-level US-Iran direct contact in 18 months on Mar 5. However, IRGC hardliner opposition and compressed domestic political timelines in both Washington and Tehran create a narrow and fragile window — making the April deadline achievable but far from certain.

key_drivers.title: Omani backchannel active
key_drivers.detail: Muscat-hosted shuttle diplomacy established a direct senior channel — reduces miscalculation risk and compresses negotiation timeline toward earlier resolution

key_drivers.title: Narrow political window
key_drivers.detail: Both US midterm campaign and Iran's presidential transition create urgency — leaders on both sides need a deliverable before domestic constraints harden

key_drivers.title: Sanctions at threshold
key_drivers.detail: Iran reserves at $12B with 45% inflation and rial down 30% YoY — economic pain pushes Tehran's SNSC toward concessions to secure sanctions relief

key_drivers.title: IRGC hardliners blocking
key_drivers.detail: IRGC controls proxy networks and key economic assets threatened by any deal — their opposition to concessions is the single largest risk of negotiation collapse

key_drivers.title: Regional normalization cover
key_drivers.detail: Saudi-Iran détente and Abraham Accords expansion give both sides political cover to frame bilateral talks as part of broader regional stabilization, lowering domestic backlash risk

watch_list.condition: If renewed → maintains economic pressure on Tehran, ↑ ceasefire incentive by ~10%
If senior officials attend → signals commitment, ↑ deal probability; if cancelled → ↓ sharply
If reformist candidates lead polls → ↑ diplomatic flexibility; hardliner surge → ↓
If not extended → escalates pressure, could accelerate or collapse talks
If enrichment data shows compliance → ↑ trust for deal; violations → ↓ and possible US escalation

polymarket.question: Major Persian Gulf military confrontation in 2026?

disclaimer: OSINT assessment. Not investment or policy advice. Subject to revision.
